coredata.dc:title: Europium-modified carbon dots-based ratiometric fluorescence probe for anthrax biomarker dipicolinic acid detection

coredata.dc:creator: Du, Wenhao
Liu, Tianhong
Yin, Jiazi
Cao, Benmei
Yu, Long
Li, Xin
Sun, Mingtai
Yuan, Chao
Wang, Suhua
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive and selective detection of 2, 6-dipicolinic acid (DPA), a biomarker secreted by Bacillus anthracis spores, is crucial in ensuring food safety. The environmental friendliness of carbon dots (CDs), the excellent chemical and optical stability of lanthanide metals, and the ease of functionalization are increasingly emerging as key research focuses in the field. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence probe was developed based on CDs functionalized with Eu3+ coordination for DPA detection. Upon the addition of DPA, the turquoise fluorescence of the probe was quenched, and a distinct red fluorescence attributed to the Eu3+ emerged due to the antenna effect, resulting in a dual-channel fluorescence response with high sensitivity and selectivity. Furthermore, a paper-based sensor was fabricated for the quantitative detection of DPA, utilizing a smartphone equipped with a color block recognition system, enabling on-site rapid detection of DPA in the range of 0–100 µM. The design goal of a logic gate is to simplify sample analysis and detect the presence or absence of DPA using a smartphone. This finding establishes a practical approach for on-site detection of DPA in real-world settings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2,6-dipicolinic acid
Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence and ultraviolet spectroscopy of CDs and CDs@Eu3+
Selectivity and anti-interference investigation of the probe to DPA
Detection of DPA in real samples
Logic gate design for DPA detection

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U21A20290, 21705054, 22276041), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2022A1515011656, 2023A1515030008), the Projects of Talents Recruitment of GDUPT (2022rcyj2002), the Department of Education of Guangdong Province (2021ZDZX4014), the Maoming City Science and Technology Plan Project (2023385), the Sci-Tech Innovation Project of Graduate School of Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology and the Sci-Tech Innovation Project for Joint Training Base of Professional Degrees Graduate of Maoming Green Chemical Industry Research Institute (2024KJCX080).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the prepared CDs. (b) X-ray diffraction patterns of PDA and CDs. (c) FTIR spectrograms of CDs. (d-f) The corresponding high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s and O 1 s of the prepared CDs.

(a) Excitation and emission spectra of the prepared CDs. Inset: images of CDs solution under UV light and sunlight. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, Eu3+, CDs@ Eu3+ and CDs@Eu3+-DPA. Inset: CDs, CDs@Eu3+ and CDs@Eu3+-DPA were photographed under a 254 nm handheld UV lamp. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CDs-DPA, CDs@Eu3+, CDs@Eu3+-DPA, DPA and Eu3+. (d) UV–vis absorption evolution of the CDs solution upon Eu3+ addition with a concentration range. (e) UV–vis absorption evolution of the probe CDs@Eu3+ solution upon DPA addition with a concentration range. (f) The fluorescence spectra of CDs and CDs-DPA.

(a) Fluorescence spectra evolution of the CDs@Eu3+ probe solution upon the addition of different concentrations of DPA. The inset shows the color change of the solution. (b) CIE diagram of the probe solution after the addition of DPA. (c) Linear correlation plot of fluorescence intensity (I615/I487) versus DPA concentration. The error bar represents the standard deviation of the measured values from three replicate experiments (n = 3). The inset shows the linear relationship between 40–240 nM DPA and fluorescence intensity (I615/I487).

(a) Schematic diagram of the use of the logic gate for simplified data processing for DPA detection. (b) A diagram depicting the relationship between DPA concentration and fluorescence intensity was determined through logical assessment. (c) The DPA concentration corresponds to the output value of the logic gate. The threshold is set at 30 µM.
Schematic illustration of DPA detection based on CDs@Eu3+.

head.ce:title: Europium-modified carbon dots-based ratiometric fluorescence probe for anthrax biomarker dipicolinic acid detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Agricultural Production and Intelligent Equipment, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology, Maoming 525000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Agricultural Production and Intelligent Equipment
School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Agricultural Production and Intelligent Equipment, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology, Maoming 525000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive and selective detection of 2, 6-dipicolinic acid (DPA), a biomarker secreted by Bacillus anthracis spores, is crucial in ensuring food safety. The environmental friendliness of carbon dots (CDs), the excellent chemical and optical stability of lanthanide metals, and the ease of functionalization are increasingly emerging as key research focuses in the field. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence probe was developed based on CDs functionalized with Eu3+ coordination for DPA detection. Upon the addition of DPA, the turquoise fluorescence of the probe was quenched, and a distinct red fluorescence attributed to the Eu3+ emerged due to the antenna effect, resulting in a dual-channel fluorescence response with high sensitivity and selectivity. Furthermore, a paper-based sensor was fabricated for the quantitative detection of DPA, utilizing a smartphone equipped with a color block recognition system, enabling on-site rapid detection of DPA in the range of 0–100 µM. The design goal of a logic gate is to simplify sample analysis and detect the presence or absence of DPA using a smartphone. This finding establishes a practical approach for on-site detection of DPA in real-world settings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Anthrax, a contagious disease, can be triggered by rotting animal carcasses, contaminated feed, contaminated water, and infected plants. Additionally, anthrax spores can cause respiratory distress, diarrhea, vomiting, and pimples through direct contact with the human body via the intestinal or respiratory tract, and can be life-threatening if not diagnosed in time [1]. Therefore, the detection of DPA, the primary metabolite of anthrax spores, is crucial for ensuring ecological and public health safety as well as for disease diagnosis. Conventional methods for DPA determination are well established and mainly include surface-enhanced Raman scattering [2], Liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [3], and hydrophilic interaction chromatography [4–6]. Although these methods are capable of accurately determining the amount of DPA, the high cost, complexity of operation, difficult maintenance, environmental constraints, and other drawbacks greatly restrict their practical application. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop rapid on-site DPA detection methods characterized by high portability, simplicity, and sensitivity.

The emergence of fluorescent materials has changed the perception of pollutant detection. Common fluorescent materials are metal–organic frameworks [7–9], Covalent Organic Framework [10–12], CDs [13], etc. Among them, lanthanide-doped fluorescent materials have emerged as a research focus in fluorescence sensing, attributed to their unique energy transfer mechanisms, tunable fluorescence signals, exceptional stability, and high luminescence efficiency [14]. Li et al. employed an Eu-Tb co-doping strategy to design a nucleotide-based bivalent lanthanide fluorescent probe and integrated machine learning to enable efficient detection of specific targets [15]. Xu et al. integrated silver nanoclusters and europium-based materials into the same nano-detection system to construct a platform enabling real-time, rapid visual analysis and intelligent detection of specific targets [16]. Chen et al. designed Eu3+-coordinated nanomaterials that enable both detection and adsorption of specific targets [17].

In this work, a CDs@Eu3+ ratiometric fluorescence probe was straightforwardly constructed by immobilizing Eu3+ on the surface of carboxyl-functionalized CDs. Owing to the strong coordination ability of Eu3+ with carboxyl groups, the probe was easily prepared by mixing the synthesized CDs with Eu3+, exhibiting turquoise fluorescence. The fluorescence emission of Eu3+ was suppressed by its surface-coordinated water molecules. In the presence of DPA, the red-channel fluorescence emission characteristic of Eu3+ is readily restored, resulting in a fluorescence shift from turquoise to yellow and finally to red. Owing to this distinct color change, the probe was further employed to construct the paper-based sensor for the quantitative determination of DPA, utilizing a smartphone for color block recognition. By introducing the logic gate to simplify data analysis, it becomes possible to determine whether the solution contains DPA. This provides a new approach for simplifying in situ online detection (Scheme 1 ).
All experimental reagents and experimental equipment used in this experiment are presented in the supporting information of the manuscript.

CDs were prepared according to a previously reported method [18]. Briefly, 240 mg of 3,9-perylenedicarboxylic acid (PDA), 4.8 g of boric acid, and 9.6 g of urea were added to a 50 mL beaker and thoroughly mixed before being incubated in an oven at 180 °C for 6 h. The resulting puffy substance was dissolved in 20 mL of water and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to separate large particles from the liquid. The supernatant was transferred into a dialysis bag with a 1,000 Dalton molecular weight cut-off and dialyzed for 36 h to remove water-soluble molecules. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to obtain orange-red CDs powder (Fig. S1).

Typically, 0.7329 g of Europium (III) chloride hexahydrate was added to 60 mL of water containing 0.0011 g of CDs for a complexation reaction at room temperature with 12 h of rotation. The reaction mixture was then dialyzed for 48 h using a dialysis bag with a 1,000 Dalton molecular weight cut-off to remove uncomplexed Eu3+. The resulting CDs@Eu3+ probe solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

To detect DPA, 990 µL of CDs@Eu3+ stock solution (0.07 mg/mL) was mixed with 10 µL of DPA solutions at various concentrations to prepare 1 mL of reaction solution with a DPA concentration range of 0.04–120 µM. For optimal effect of CDs and Eu3+, the pH of the CDs@Eu3+ solution was adjusted to an alkaline pH. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 251 nm after a 5-minute incubation in a cuvette at room temperature.

To evaluate the selectivity of the probe to DPA, the photoluminescence response of the CDs@Eu3+ to compounds with structural similarity to DPA (2,3-Pyrimidinedicarboxylic acid (2,3-PA), 2,4-Pyrimidinedicarboxylic acid (2,4-PA), 2,5-Pyrimidinedicarboxylic acid (2,5-PA)), as well as to some common metal ions (Zn2+, Ni2+, Na+, Mn2+, K+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Ba2+ and Al3+) and anions (S-, NO2 –, HS-, HPO4 2-, HCO3 –, CO3 2–, CH3COO– and Br-) were investigated. The effect of coexisting interfering analytes with DPA was examined using the same concentration of interfering ions as DPA, all the protocols are the same as those for DPA detection.

To prepare the paper-based sensors, commercial water-based filter paper was immersed in a petri dish containing the prepared CDs@Eu3+ stock solution for 5 min, followed by drying in an oven for 2 h at 50 °C for further use. For DPA detection, 10 µL of DPA solution of various concentrations (0–100 µM) was dropped on the surface of the CDs@Eu3+ paper-based sensor for 5 min incubation, followed by exposure to a UV lamp with excitation wavelength at 254 nm for fluorescence collection. Three paper-based substrates were fabricated with gradient concentrations, with triplicate image sets acquired through a smartphone color block recognition system analysis platform to ensure measurement reproducibility. The R/G values were then collected and compared to the regression curve to determine the concentration of DPA.

In this work, a one-step dry heat synthesis method was used for CDs preparation, in which PDA, boric acid and urea play the role of conjugated building blocks, reaction medium, and catalyst and foaming agent, respectively [18]. Before the reaction, the well-mixed reactants appeared as granules. After the reaction and purification, the product had a fluffy texture and appeared as an orange solid under sunlight (Fig. S2). The obtained CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), crystal diffraction (XRD), Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 1 a is the image of the transmission electron microscopy analysis of the CDs, which shows the good monodispersity of the obtained CDs. XRD analysis shows that the characteristic PDA peaks were distinctly observed at 12.5°, 24.3°, 26.3°, 28.3°, 40.5°, and 50.1° (Fig. 1b). This is consistent with the results of previous reports [18]. In contrast, the XRD peaks of the synthesized CDs were predominantly located within the range of 10° to 35°, with the disappearance of the original peaks associated with PDA. This shift can be attributed to the steric hindrance between the sp2 domains in the CDs core, further confirming the successful synthesis of CDs. Fig. 1c shows the FTIR spectrum of the prepared CDs, in which the peaks at 3431 cm−1 and 3018 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of –OH [19,20]. The stretching vibration of C=O is responsible for the sharp absorption peak at 1640 cm−1 [21], and the absorption peaks of aromatic C=C groups at 1590 cm−1 and 1404 cm−1 reveal that the CDs have a good conjugated structure [22,23]. In addition, the prepared CDs display a stretching vibration of the C=C bond at 766 cm−1 [24], while the absorption peaks at 1235 cm−1 and 1112 cm−1 belong to C-O stretching vibrations. XPS analysis reveals that the elemental composition of the synthesized CDs includes C, N, and O (Fig. 1d). Furthermore, it is clear to identify the distinct peaks at 289.1 eV, 286.8 eV, and 284.8 eV in the XPS spectra of C 1 s, which correspond to C=O, C-O, and C-C bonds [25], respectively (Fig. 1e). There is no doubt that C-O and C=O are responsible for the two peaks in graph f that are present at 533.7 eV and 532.5 eV [26] (Fig. 1f). Raman spectrum (Fig. S3) shows the sp2 hybridized structure of C at 1575 cm−1 and an interring C-C stretching vibration at 1293 cm−1 [27,28], further confirming the successful synthesis of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and ultraviolet spectroscopy of CDs and CDs@Eu3+
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1, a large number of carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs coordinate with Eu3+, and the presence of the medium facilitates the interaction between Eu3+ and water, preventing the red fluorescence production of Eu3+ (Fig. S4). When irradiated with a 254 nm UV lamp, the solution exhibits turquoise fluorescence (peak at 487 nm). Upon the addition of DPA, which replaces the Eu3+coordination with water, the antenna effect causes the fluorescence to shift from turquoise to red (peak at 615 nm). The optical excitation wavelength of the prepared CDs is centered at 251 nm, as shown in the excitation spectrum, with its fluorescence spectra peaking at approximately 487 nm under an excitation wavelength range of 245 to 251 nm (Fig. 2 a). Optically, the prepared CDs exhibited turquoise fluorescence under excitation at 254 nm. For the preparation of the DPA probe, Eu3+ was introduced onto the surface of the CDs through a coordination reaction between Eu3+ and the carboxyl groups on the material's surface. As expected, the unique fluorescence of Eu3+ coordinated on the surface of CDs was suppressed by chelated water molecules, resulting in the fluorescence color of CDs@Eu3+ being identical to that of the original CDs (Fig. 2b). Additionally, the surface-coordinated Eu3+ partially quenched the fluorescence of the generated CDs (Fig. S5). However, in the presence of the target DPA, fluorescence associated with Eu3+ in the mixture of CDs@Eu3+ and DPA became prominent, as evidenced by a distinct fluorescence color change from turquoise to red. This phenomenon can be attributed to a substitution reaction between DPA and water molecules chelated with Eu3+, as confirmed by the coordination reaction between DPA and Eu3+ [29]. DPA restored the fluorescence of Eu3+, as evidenced by the fluorescence curve and the fluorescence color evolution (red curve and inset photo in Fig. 2b). There was a significant peak change at 275 nm with the addition of DPA compared to CDs@Eu3+, indicating the formation of a chelate between CDs@Eu3+ and DPA (Fig. 2c). To further validate the conjecture, varying concentrations of Eu3+ were introduced into the CDs solution (Fig. 2d). A decrease in the absorption at the characteristic peak of CDs was observed, attributed to the strong chelating ability of Eu3+, which promotes CDs aggregation (Fig. S6). Upon adding DPA to the system, the gradual evolution of UV absorption confirmed the emergence of double peaks (Fig. 2e). As shown in Fig. 2f, fluorescence analysis of the CDs-DPA revealed no detectable interaction, as evidenced by the absence of an emission peak at 615 nm. This observation confirms that the specific interaction between the components exclusively requires the presence of Eu3+.

The chromatic transition mechanism of CDs@Eu3+ upon DPA incorporation was investigated through systematic analysis of probe-analyte interactions. As illustrated in Fig. S7, the photophysical process initiates with DPA-mediated photon absorption, promoting electrons from the ground state (S0) to the excited singlet state (S1). Subsequently, the metastable S1 state undergoes spontaneous intersystem crossing (ISC) to populate the lower-energy triplet state (T1). Energy transfer occurs when the T1 state energy level exceeds the 5D0 excited state of Eu3+, enabling efficient directional energy migration from the organic ligand (T1) to the lanthanide ion (5D0). It emits red fluorescence when electrons transition from the excited state of Eu3+ back to the ground state. Fluorescence spectra confirmed that DPA exhibited no fluorescence peaks, while the probe displayed fluorescence peaks exclusively in the 400–500 nm range (Fig. S8). The suppression of the characteristic peaks of Eu3+ was attributed to the high-frequency vibrations of hydroxyl radicals in water molecules. After the addition of DPA, the fluorescence peaks of the probe at 593, 618, 652, and 697 nm were attributed to the Eu3+ transitions of 5D0→7F1, 5D0→7F2, 5D0→7F3, and 5D0→7F4, respectively (Fig. S9). Due to its strong chelation ability, DPA can displace the coordinated water molecules surrounding Eu3+, thereby mitigating the non-radiative energy loss typically associated with these water molecules [30,31]. This Alteration elucidates the observed shift in fluorescence color from green to red within the detection system upon the addition of DPA.

Additionally, an overlap was observed between the UV–vis absorption peak of DPA and the fluorescence excitation peak of CDs@Eu3+ (Fig. S10), suggesting that an internal filtering effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect might exist between CDs@Eu3+ and DPA. Comparative analysis of the UV–vis absorption spectra for CDs@Eu3+, DPA, and their composite system revealed no emerging absorption bands in the hybrid phase relative to the individual components (Fig. S11). This spectral consistency confirms the absence of chemical bond formation between CDs@Eu3+ and DPA, thereby excluding FRET as the fluorescence enhancement mechanism. The observed emission amplification in the presence of DPA is therefore primarily ascribed to the IFE, where DPA acts as a light-absorbing chromophore, altering the excitation-emission profile through competitive photon absorption. It has been confirmed in the relevant literature[31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference investigation of the probe to DPA
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity and anti-interference are critical metrics for evaluating the performance of CDs@Eu3+ in detecting DPA. To investigate the selectivity of CDs@Eu3+ in environments with diverse elemental species, common metal ions (Zn2+, Ni2+, Na+, Mn2+, K+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Ba2+ and Al3+) and anions (S-, NO2 –, HS-, HPO4 2-, HCO3 –, CO3 2–, CH3COO– and Br-) were used to evaluate the performance of probe for DPA detection. Fig. S12a validates the hypothesis. The same concentration of interfering substances was added to the CDs@Eu3+ solution. It was observed that most of the I615/I487 values for the ions fluctuated by no more than 0.1. In contrast, the ratio of antibiotics to structural analogs fluctuates around 0.2. Fig. S12b illustrates that the addition of interfering ions caused a change in the fluorescence intensity at 487 nm, without the generation of any additional fluorescence peaks. As for the antibiotics (CAF, TTC, OTC, NA, DOH, USP) and DPA structural analogs ((2,3-PA), (2,4-PA), (2,5-PA)), it is noted that the fluorescence interference from 2,5-PA cannot be ignored, although the presence of other analogs and those antibiotics did not bring obvious interference to the probe solution (Fig. S12c). Fig. S12d validates our hypothesis. The same concentration of interfering substances was added to the CDs@Eu3+ solution containing DPA. It was observed that most of the I615/I487 values for the ions fluctuated by no more than 0.1. In contrast, the ratios for antibiotics and structural analogs exceeded 1, as their structural complexity significantly influenced the probe's fluorescence intensity.

Considering the various factors influencing the DPA assay, the reaction time was set to 5 min based on the time-stability study of the probe solution (Fig. S13), while the pH of the probe solution was maintained at 8.0 based on the result that the probe solution displayed the maximum fluorescence (Fig. S14) and the fact that the pH value of most of the environmental sample are close to neutral. The sensitivity of the CDs@Eu3+ probe to DPA was assessed by tracking the fluorescence emission intensity upon the addition of different concentrations of DPA to the probe solution. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the emission of Eu3+ at 615 nm gradually increases with rising DPA concentrations, whereas the fluorescence emission of CDs at 487 nm is suppressed by DPA, exhibiting a decreasing trend. Consequently, the probe solution exhibited a fluorescence color change from turquoise to red, which could be further evidenced by the CIE diagram (Fig. 3b). The x-axis interval is 0.6, facilitating visualization and analysis. Fig. 3c illustrates the correlation between varying concentrations of DPA and the fluorescence ratio I615/I487. Using the criteria of three times the deviation of the blank signal. It is worth mentioning that the high-concentration DPA fluorescence ratios fitted showed a non-linear correlation. The DPA concentration and ratio can be expressed as Y = 1.0710e0.0310x-0.6590 with an R2 of 0.994. Upon reducing the DPA concentration, a clear linear correlation was observed, with an R2 value of 0.995. The detection limit of the probe was determined to be as low as 7.566 nM, which compares favorably with previously reported methods (Table S1). Based on the results above, the CDs@Eu3+ solution proposed in this paper demonstrates high visual selectivity for DPA detection, offering a potential solution for on-site qualitative identification of DPA.

The ultimate objective of environmental analysis in practical applications is the development of portable and reliable sensors characterized by their ability to perform instant on-site detection. In this work, the paper-based sensor was constructed by immobilization of the CDs@Eu3+ probe on commercial filter paper by the common immersing method, and a smartphone installed with the color block recognition system was used for imaging and data analysis (Fig. S15). Various DPA solution concentrations (20 µL, 0–100 µM) were applied to the paper sensor and underwent incubation for five minutes, followed by irradiation with a hand-held 254 nm UV lamp to keep the handheld UV lamp about 10 cm away from the paper base for imaging collection by smartphone in the dark. As shown in Fig. S15a, an obvious fluorescence color evolution on the paper could be observed in the DPA concentration range from 0-100 µM. Even at the DPA concentration of 40 µM, a distinguishable color against the blank could be obtained by the naked eye. Fig. S15b further shows the plot of the R/G ratio against the DPA concentration, which reveals a linear regression equation with R2 of 0.982. That result indicated that the proposed paper-based sensor has the potential for DPA instant on-site quantitative detection in practical applications. Recent developments in DPA paper-based sensors are summarized in Table S2. Notably, the highly sensitive CDs@Eu3+ paper-based sensors exhibit exceptional sensitivity and accuracy, making them a promising tool for DPA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DPA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality and feasibility of the paper-based sensors for the detection of DPA were further validated using real samples including tap water, lake water, and milk. Before testing, all samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min to remove large impurities and then filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to eliminate larger particulates. The samples were subsequently spiked to achieve two DPA concentrations (30 and 60 µM). Triplicate paper-based substrates were fabricated for each experimental group, including control experiments with blank substrates, followed by statistical analysis to calculate mean values and standard deviations. It is worth mentioning that no observable fluorescence enhancement was observed in the samples without DPA spiking. The results, shown in Table S3, revealed that all recoveries for DPA-spiked samples were close to 100 % (ranging from 91 % to 100.17 %). In addition, it is found that the obtained data from liquid-based and paper-based analysis were quite matched, demonstrating the potential of the prepared sensor in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Logic gate design for DPA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The logic gate can simplify complex data into signal outputs, making them widely applicable in the field of signal processing. In this study, a simple logic gate decoder was designed using logical operations to determine the presence of DPA in the test sample and whether its concentration exceeded the threshold (Fig. 4 a). The final result can be directly transmitted to a mobile phone, eliminating the need for further data processing. In short, the logic gate is designed with two levels, each scanned three times. The first level identifies whether the solution contains DPA, while the second level determines if the DPA content exceeds the standard threshold. In the first level, the signals “1″ and ”0″ are defined as indicating the presence and absence of DPA, respectively. The judgment of the secondary result is based on the F615/F487 ratio. The threshold value is set at 30 µM based on the minimum concentration that could result in an obvious fluorescent color change from blue to red (Fig. 4b). “1″ and ”0″ in the second level represent DPA measurements greater than and less than the threshold, respectively. The test solution was scanned three times at an excitation wavelength of 254 nm following the aforementioned logic rules. The absence of DPA in the test solution results in an output of (0, 0). The presence of DPA in the test solution, with a concentration not exceeding the threshold, results in an output of (1, 0), while the presence of DPA above the threshold results in an output of (1, 1) (Fig. 4c). The design of the logic gate simplifies the post-processing of test data, enabling the experimenter to detect DPA efficiently and quickly.

In summary, a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe based on Eu3+-coordinated CDs is reported for the detection of DPA, a metabolite of Bacillus anthracis. The fluorescence emission associated with Eu3+ in the red channel is restored in the presence of the target due to DPA's strong coordination ability with Eu3+, while the initial fluorescence in the blue channel is slightly suppressed. This dual-channel fluorescence responsive phenomenon leads to the high detection sensitivity of the probe for DPA assay with the detection limit of 7.566 nM, with satisfying selectivity. In addition, the probe is applied to a paper substrate, which can be used to construct the paper-based sensor for semi-quantitative analysis of DPA within a linear range of 0 to 100 µM, enabling on-site detection of DPA. The application of the logic gate simplifies subsequent data processing and significantly reduces processing time. This demonstrates that the fabricated nanoprobes have significant practical value in field detection applications.

Wenhao Du: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Conceptualization. Tianhong Liu: Validation, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis. Jiazi Yin: Validation, Software. Benmei Cao: Writing – review & editing. Long Yu: Validation, Software. Xin Li: Validation, Software. Mingtai Sun: Funding acquisition. Chao Yuan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Suhua Wang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U21A20290, 21705054, 22276041), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2022A1515011656, 2023A1515030008), the Projects of Talents Recruitment of GDUPT (2022rcyj2002), the Department of Education of Guangdong Province (2021ZDZX4014), the Maoming City Science and Technology Plan Project (2023385), the Sci-Tech Innovation Project of Graduate School of Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology and the Sci-Tech Innovation Project for Joint Training Base of Professional Degrees Graduate of Maoming Green Chemical Industry Research Institute (2024KJCX080).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.114058.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One Health Assessment of Bacillus Anthracis Incidence and Detection in Anthrax-Endemic Areas of Pakistan
